subject: ARTHIST
course_description: Possible topics include Post-Impressionism and its consequences; Matisse & Picasso; Art and Politics between the Wars; Dada and Surrealism; the Avant-Garde; Abstract Art; What is Art?; Theories of Modernism. May be repeated for credit when topic changes, up to a maximum of twelve hours.
course_notes: (Same as PHIL 385 1) By the late 20th century, architects and urbanists' unlikely exchanges with philosophers had become intense, prolific, radical, innovative, and widespread like never before. Their roles overlapped as they read one another, talked to each other, and at times even worked together. They produced iconic essays, projects, conferences, debates, and collaborations that fundamentally transformed architectural and urban discourse and practice alike. But what did designers and philosophers have to gain from crossing disciplinary borders? How have philosophical notions influenced architecture and urbanism? How have designers contributed to philosophical thought? In this seminar, we will dive into the theories and ideas that animated these exchanges. Understand their stories, actors, contexts, approaches, methodologies, proposals, and their effects then and today. Art History major/minor: Division III Architectural Studies minor: elective
